channel.title: Drupal CMS
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal CMS.

item.title: #3591409: Unable to add components to any Canvas pages in any site template
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3591409 · 1 contributor · 5 comments

Summary

On a fresh Drupal CMS installation, attempting to edit any Canvas page produced a fatal "Error 500: A Component is being normalized that belongs in no Canvas UI library." This made Canvas completely unusable on all site templates including Haven and Starter. The fix adds noUi: true to the two Single Directory Components (SDCs) provided by the installer theme, so they are correctly excluded from Canvas's component library and no longer cause an error after installation is complete.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The drupal_cms_installer_theme is only present during the installation process and is removed after the site is set up. However, its two SDC components, dialog and language-switcher, were being discovered by Canvas's component plugin manager after installation. Because their provider theme no longer existed, Canvas could not normalize them and threw a LogicException. Canvas respects the noUi: true flag in a component's .component.yml metadata to exclude that component from its UI library entirely. Adding noUi: true to both drupal_cms_installer/theme/components/dialog/dialog.component.yml and drupal_cms_installer/theme/components/language-switcher/language-switcher.component.yml prevents Canvas from attempting to load or display these installer-only components.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 26, 2026

Key contributors

  • gábor hojtsy (Acquia)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3483394: Build privacy advanced recipe
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 10 files, +150/−0 · yml +92 · php +31 · json +15 · gitignore +12
  • Discussion: #3483394 · 5 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 9

Summary

A new optional drupal_cms_privacy_advanced recipe is proposed for Drupal CMS, intended for sites that allow user account registration and need to meet data protection obligations. When applied, it installs the GDPR contrib module suite and configures it to: mark user account fields as personally identifiable (PII), add an "All your data" tab in user profiles where authenticated users can submit Subject Access Requests (SARs) and data removal requests, provide admins a task queue for processing those requests, and anonymize personal data in SQL database dumps so staging or development environments do not contain real user data. The recipe is designed as an optional complement to the basic privacy recipe. The MR was closed without merging; discussion in the thread shifted toward first extracting the underlying GDPR module components into standalone first-class modules before building the recipe on top of them.

Impact

Affects module maintainers and site owners.

  • Permission change: Authenticated users gain create gdpr tasks, view gdpr data summary, and view gdpr tasks permissions when the recipe is applied.
  • New API: New drupal_cms_privacy_advanced recipe enables PII field marking, Subject Access Requests, data removal requests, and SQL dump anonymization for user accounts via the GDPR contrib module suite.

Technical details

The recipe (recipes/drupal_cms_privacy_advanced) installs four GDPR module sub-modules: gdpr, gdpr_fields, gdpr_dump, and gdpr_tasks. Because these sub-modules do not provide config schema, the recipe cannot use config actions and instead uses config.strict: false with raw YAML config files placed in config/ alongside the recipe.

The gdpr_fields config marks the name, mail, pass, and init fields on the user bundle as PII, each with rta: inc (include in SARs output) and rtf: anonymize (anonymize on removal requests), using username_anonymizer, email_anonymizer, or password_anonymizer as appropriate. The gdpr_dump config maps those same columns in users_field_data to the matching anonymizers for SQL dump sanitization. The gdpr_tasks config sets the export directory to private://forget, which requires a configured Drupal private filesystem to function. The recipe grants authenticated users the create gdpr tasks, view gdpr data summary, and view gdpr tasks permissions. A ComponentValidationTest verifies clean apply and idempotency.

Hands-on testing by boromino (comment #15883565) surfaced blockers: exports fail silently when no private file path is configured in settings.php, the password hash is exported in SARs output, and there is no guard against processing a removal request for user 1. These issues, combined with concerns about the gdpr brand being EU-centric, prompted jurgenhaas to propose (comment #15959008) extracting gdpr_fields and gdpr_tasks into a standalone "PII API" module with separate, narrowly scoped use-case modules for SARs, JSON:API protection, and render output protection, informed by a conversation with yanniboi, the GDPR sub-module maintainer. The MR was closed pending that upstream work.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 25, 2024

Key contributors

  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • boromino (LakeDrops)
  • mxr576 (Pronovix)
  • yanniboi (FreelyGive)

Collaboration

boromino applied the recipe manually and reported four concrete problems in comment #15883565: exports failed when the private file path was not set, password hashes appeared in SARs output, user 1 could be subjected to data removal, and the Content Links config lacked a default link to the privacy policy page. These findings were substantive enough to redirect the overall approach. jurgenhaas then described (comment #15959008) a broader architectural shift discussed in a privacy track meeting with yanniboi (the GDPR sub-module maintainer): rather than wrapping the existing GDPR module as-is, the team would extract gdpr_fields and gdpr_tasks into independent "PII API" and use-case modules before building the Drupal CMS recipe on top of them.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3583960: Replace friendlycaptcha with ALTCHA
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3583960 · 4 contributors · 16 comments
  • Followers: 7

Summary

The drupal_cms_anti_spam and drupal_cms_starter recipes now install the ALTCHA module instead of FriendlyCaptcha. The switch was triggered by FriendlyCaptcha dropping local-only (no external calls) verification support in its 2.x branch, which is the feature Drupal CMS requires. ALTCHA's default mode is fully self-hosted with no third-party calls, keeps the CAPTCHA secret in Drupal's State API (never exported to config), and ships its JavaScript widget as a bundled Web Component that requires no manual library installation. No upgrade path is provided for existing sites; site owners already using the anti-spam recipe will need to manually uninstall FriendlyCaptcha and install ALTCHA.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Config change: New altcha.settings config file added to the recipe; friendlycaptcha.settings config action and the site_uuid recipe input removed.

Technical details

The drupal_cms_anti_spam recipe removes friendlycaptcha from its install list and adds altcha, updates captcha.settings to set default_challenge: altcha/ALTCHA, and removes both the friendlycaptcha.settings config action and the input block that previously injected a site_uuid to generate placeholder API keys for FriendlyCaptcha's local endpoint. A new altcha.settings config file is included in the recipe with integration_type: self_hosted and all other fields left at defaults. The drupal_cms_starter recipe likewise swaps friendlycaptcha for altcha in its install list. A FriendlyCaptcha-specific assertion (buttonExists('Click to start verification')) is removed from ContactFormTest as ALTCHA's widget does not present a manual trigger button.

Upgrade

Drupal CMS does not ship upgrade paths between recipe versions. To switch an existing site from FriendlyCaptcha to ALTCHA:

  1. Install ALTCHA and configure it via /admin/config/people/captcha/altcha.
  2. Update the default CAPTCHA challenge at /admin/config/people/captcha to altcha/ALTCHA.
  3. Uninstall FriendlyCaptcha.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: April 9, 2026

Key contributors

  • jurgenhaas (LakeDrops)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • poker10 (ActivIT s.r.o.)

Collaboration

When poker10 questioned whether ALTCHA was mature and effective enough to replace FriendlyCaptcha, jurgenhaas responded with a detailed side-by-side comparison of both modules covering PoW algorithms, difficulty scaling, secret storage, replay protection, and false-positive/negative rates. The analysis concluded that FriendlyCaptcha's local mode offered stronger baseline bot resistance (~200x more computational work per challenge) but that ALTCHA had a cleaner security architecture. pameeela cut through the debate by noting that the comparison was beside the point: FriendlyCaptcha no longer meets the project's core requirement of local-only verification, so the choice was ALTCHA or some other module, not ALTCHA vs. FriendlyCaptcha.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3499319: Reponsive images should use sizes attribute for container-width sensitive image loading
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal unknown (0)
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3499319 · 2 contributors · 6 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

Drupal CMS responsive image styles now use the HTML sizes attribute approach instead of mapping a single image style per breakpoint. All aspect-ratio-specific styles (e.g. 16_9_medium, 3_2_large, 4_3_wide) emit a srcset of multiple focal-point-cropped WebP candidates and a sizes: 100vw hint, letting the browser pick the right resolution automatically. The responsive image styles are also decoupled from the Olivero theme: they now depend on custom breakpoints (custom.sm through custom.xl) defined and managed through an ECA model, so Olivero no longer needs to be installed just for image configuration to work.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

All responsive_image.styles.* configs shipped by drupal_cms_media and drupal_cms_starter use image_mapping_type: sizes under the custom.sm breakpoint with sizes: 100vw. Each style lists between one and five focal-point-cropped WebP sizes_image_styles at different pixel widths; the browser resolves which file to request based on its own viewport arithmetic. Hero styles (hero_large, hero_wide) continue to use image_mapping_type: image_style because they have a fixed layout that spans known breakpoints rather than a fluid container.

The breakpoint group was previously olivero, requiring Olivero to be installed alongside the image recipe. MR623 (tracking issue #3543740) introduced an ECA model (eca.eca.define_breakpoints) that fires on eca_render:breakpoints_alter and injects four custom.* breakpoints at runtime via eca_render_alter_breakpoint actions. All responsive image styles were updated to reference breakpoint_id: custom.sm and breakpoint_group: custom, with an enforced config dependency on the ECA model so that deleting the model cascades to delete the styles. The recipe's install list replaced olivero with bpmn_io, eca_base, eca_render, eca_ui, and modeler_api.

A related simplification (part of the same effort) removed the "large" view mode options (16_9_large, 1_1_square_large, etc.) from the CKEditor allowed embedded media display modes, since a narrower content column means "large" in-body images are no longer meaningfully distinct from "medium."

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2025

Collaboration

tonypaulbarker commented that the editor's explicit size choice when embedding an image (small vs. medium in a narrow column) cannot be replaced by the sizes attribute alone, because responsive images decide based on viewport/container math rather than editorial intent. This shaped the outcome: the sizes approach was adopted for resolution selection within each named style, but the distinct small/medium/large/wide view modes were kept so editors retain control over the display size they intend.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3591375: Automatically copy font and color CSS files to Drupal root if a site template sets up a Mercury-based theme by default
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3591375 · 1 contributor · 2 comments

Summary

When a site template sets a Mercury-based theme as the site default, Drupal CMS Helper now automatically copies that theme's non-minified CSS files (typically theme.css and fonts.css) from the theme directory into the Drupal web root. Because Canvas does not yet offer a UI for overriding fonts and colors, Mercury-based themes expose these as plain CSS files that developers can edit. Placing copies in the web root gives developers an obvious, version-control-friendly location to make those customizations without touching the theme package itself. The copy operation uses FileExists::Error, so it will not overwrite files a developer has already customized. If the web root is not writable, or the files already exist, the copy attempt is silently skipped.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New static RecipeSubscriber::isMercuryBased(Extension $theme): bool method for checking if a theme is Mercury or a Mercury sub-theme.

Technical details

The logic is added to RecipeSubscriber::onApply() in drupal_cms_helper. After handling the existing front-page alias and user-cache concerns, it now also runs when $event->recipe->type === 'Site'. It reads the current default theme from system.theme config, calls the new static RecipeSubscriber::isMercuryBased(Extension $theme) helper (which returns true when the theme machine name is mercury or when its generator info key starts with mercury:), and only proceeds if the web root ($appRoot) is writable. It then calls LibraryDiscoveryInterface::getLibraryByName() to retrieve the theme's global library, filters its css entries to those where minified is empty/false, and copies each file to $appRoot . DIRECTORY_SEPARATOR . basename($origin) via FileSystemInterface::copy() with FileExists::Error. A caught FileException is silently swallowed so the recipe apply does not fail on a pre-existing file. Four new constructor arguments are injected via autowiring: ThemeExtensionList, FileSystemInterface, LibraryDiscoveryInterface, and the app.root parameter. A new kernel test class RecipeSubscriberTest covers both the Mercury-theme path (expects fonts.css and theme.css to be present and byte-for-byte identical to the theme source) and the non-Mercury path (expects no CSS files copied).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 27, 2026

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3580694: The project template should always place config outside the web root by default
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +21/−5 · txt +10 · php +8/−5 · json +3
  • Diff: 1accade
  • Discussion: #3580694 · 1 contributor · 6 comments
  • Followers: 3

Summary

The Drupal CMS project template now places the configuration sync directory outside the web root by default. New sites created from the template will store exported configuration in a config/sync directory at the project root, preventing direct web access to configuration files. This follows Drupal security best practices. Existing sites are not affected.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Config change: New projects place config sync directory at ../config/sync (outside web root) by default

Technical details

The project template already uses a relocated web root at web/. This change adds a config/sync directory as a sibling to the web root, keeping configuration files outside the publicly accessible directory tree.

The implementation uses the drupal-scaffold Composer plugin's file-mapping feature to append settings to default.settings.php. A new assets/append-default.settings.php.txt file contains PHP code that sets $settings['config_sync_directory'] to realpath('../config/sync') if that directory exists. The template includes an empty config/sync directory with a .gitkeep file.

The test suite in SiteTemplateInstallTest.php was updated to mirror the new assets/ directory along with composer.json. The test switched from using copy() to Filesystem::mirror() with a Finder to include both the assets directory and composer.json file.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 21, 2026
  • Committed: March 24, 2026 (2 days and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • brianperry
  • dorficus (HeroDevs)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3579163: Add support for listing paid site templates in the installer
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Closed (fixed)
  • Changes: 6 files, +252/−35 · php +236/−35 · yml +16
  • Diff: 689f589
  • Discussion: #3579163 · 3 contributors · 44 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

The Drupal CMS installer now supports paid (premium) site templates alongside free ones. Premium templates display a price and a "Buy for $X" link that opens the vendor's purchase page in a new tab. After purchasing, the user enters a license key through a modal dialog; the installer validates the key against the vendor's Composer repository (and optionally a lightweight server-side validation endpoint), saves it as a bearer token in auth.json, and then proceeds with installation as normal. Free templates also receive a "Free" badge for visual parity. The blank starter template is always listed first and pre-selected. Site hosts and testing environments can place a site-templates.php file in the site directory to override the remotely curated template list entirely; this is documented as an official extension point.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New site-templates.php extension point in the site directory: returning an iterable of template arrays overrides the remote site-templates.yml list in the Drupal CMS installer.

Technical details

The central addition is the SiteTemplate final readonly value object (Drupal\drupal_cms_installer\SiteTemplate, marked @internal). It normalizes both locally present recipes and remotely hosted Composer packages into a single shape, carrying: name, description, locator (file path or Composer package name), repository, authorization, links (as Link objects), price, purchaseUrl, keyValidationUrl, and a getScreenshot() method that base64-encodes local .webp files so they can be served from outside the web root. The static factory SiteTemplate::createFromRecipe() wraps an existing Recipe object.

SiteTemplateForm was refactored to work with SiteTemplate instances throughout. The access_key field is now a #type => container holding one textfield per premium template, each shown conditionally via #states when its associated radio is selected. Validation logic moved from submitForm() into validateForm(): alternate Composer repositories are registered via ComposerExecutor::execute('repository', 'add', ...) using the xxh3 hash of the host+port as the repository name, the bearer token is written via composer config bearer.<host> <key>, then composer show --all <package> confirms the package is visible; on failure the token is unset and a form error is set. The site directory may also contain a site-templates.php returning an iterable of template arrays; when present it overrides the remote site-templates.yml list entirely and is treated as @api.

The form_element__site_template Twig template was substantially expanded to render links, price, purchase button, and a "Already purchased? Enter license key" button that opens a <dialog> element. The premium-helper.js behavior manages the dialog lifecycle, applies a UUID input mask, performs debounced server-side validation (via Drupal.debounce and a fetch() against keyValidationUrl), and updates status indicators (loading spinner, valid checkmark, invalid X). The site-templates.yml format was extended with purchase.price, purchase.url, purchase.validation_url, links, and package.authorization keys. A new SiteTemplateInstallTest build test provides end-to-end coverage of the bearer-token flow using an embedded PHP server that simulates a protected Composer repository.

Upgrade

Site hosts who want to override the installer's template list can create a file at sites/default/site-templates.php (or whichever site directory is in use) that returns an iterable of template arrays:

<?php
// sites/default/site-templates.php
return [
  'my_template' => [
    'name' => 'My Template',
    'description' => 'A custom site template.',
    'screenshot' => 'https://example.com/screenshot.webp',
    'package' => [
      'name' => 'vendor/my-template',
      'repository' => 'https://packages.example.com',
      'authorization' => 'bearer',
    ],
    'purchase' => [
      'price' => 99,
      'url' => 'https://example.com/buy',
      'validation_url' => 'https://example.com/validate', // optional
    ],
    'links' => [
      ['text' => 'Demo', 'url' => 'https://example.com/demo'],
    ],
  ],
];

When this file exists and returns an iterable, it replaces the remote site-templates.yml list entirely. This is an official extension point (@api).

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 13, 2026
  • First commit: March 14, 2026 (1 day later)
  • Last commit: March 17, 2026 (3 days and 14 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • mherchel (Dripyard)
  • andyg5000 (Dripyard)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)

Collaboration

phenaproxima led the backend architecture across multiple merge requests, writing the SiteTemplate value object, the Composer integration, and the end-to-end build tests. mherchel drove the frontend: the modal dialog UX, the UUID input masking, and the JavaScript state machine for license key validation. pameeela reviewed the design in a Zoom call early on and contributed the final round of CSS styling tweaks (commit ab79bb57). The thread also notes that andyg5000 (Dripyard) was consulted during the design phase and provided the initial use-case requirements for paid templates.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3579729: Add an `overwrite` option to `drush site:export`
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +45/−10 · php +45/−10
  • Diff: 73ee652
  • Discussion: #3579729 · 1 contributor · 6 comments
  • Followers: 1

Summary

The drush site:export command now supports an --overwrite option that allows you to re-export a site to an existing directory. Previously, the command would fail if the destination directory already existed, requiring manual deletion before exporting again. This makes it easier to iterate on site exports during development without manually cleaning up directories between runs.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The SiteExportCommand class in the drupal_cms_helper module now accepts an --overwrite option. When this option is provided, the command skips the check that would normally fail if the destination directory exists.

The underlying SiteExporter::copyBaseRecipe() method was also updated to exclude config and recipe.yml from the base recipe when mirroring files, in addition to the existing content exclusion. This ensures these key files are always regenerated during export. The delete option was removed from the Filesystem::mirror() call, which means overwriting will only update files that are part of the export, not delete everything in the destination first. This preserves any additional files in the destination directory (like .git directories from version control).

The test coverage confirms that overwriting replaces exported files like recipe.yml while leaving untouched files like .git directories intact.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 17, 2026
  • Committed: March 17, 2026

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3579616: The site:export command should always use drupal_cms_site_template_base as a base, if available
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 9 files, +161/−203 · php +161/−203
  • Diff: c72081f
  • Discussion: #3579616 · 1 contributor · 7 comments
  • Followers: 2

Summary

The site export command now defaults to using the Drupal CMS site template starter kit as a base, making it easier to create new site templates. Running drush site:export with no arguments produces a complete, working recipe with documentation, tests, and a screenshot. The exported recipe is self-contained and does not depend on other recipes, simplifying the site template creation workflow for DrupalCon Chicago and the Drupal CMS marketplace.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The site:export command previously allowed exporting to any base recipe and attempted to merge changes into existing recipe files. This approach was overcomplicated and error-prone for the primary use case: creating new site templates for the Drupal CMS marketplace.

The command now automatically uses drupal_cms_site_template_base (the site template starter kit) as the default base if available. Both the Drush command and the UI export controller check for the starter kit's existence using a new SiteExporter::getRecipePath() method, which determines where Composer installs recipes by parsing the project's composer.json.

The export process now completely overwrites recipe.yml and composer.json rather than merging changes. The generated recipe.yml contains only the site name, type, installed extensions, and config actions—no references to other recipes. The composer.json file is rebuilt from scratch with requirements for all installed extensions, removing any starter kit development dependencies.

This produces monolithic site templates that are self-contained and ready to publish. The export includes all content, configuration, documentation, tests, and supporting files from the starter kit (README, CI configuration, screenshots), but excludes the starter kit's sample content.

The installer form no longer stores the selected base template in state, since the export functionality now handles this automatically.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 16, 2026
  • Committed: March 17, 2026

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3575283: Add the ability to export a site template as a ZIP file
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Closed (fixed)
  • Changes: 3 files, +75/−2 · php +75/−2
  • Diff: dfd34d6
  • Discussion: #3575283 · 4 contributors · 25 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Drupal CMS now ships a dedicated route at /admin/config/development/site-export (inside the drupal_cms_helper module) that exports the current site as a downloadable ZIP file containing a Drupal recipe. The route requires the administer site configuration permission and is intentionally not linked anywhere in the UI. Individual site templates, such as the "Blank" starter kit, can expose it by adding their own menu links. Sites installed from the blank starter kit (drupal_cms_site_template_base) will have their export automatically seeded from that base template, including scaffold files like README.md and CI configuration.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New route drupal_cms_helper.site_export at /admin/config/development/site-export (marked @api); SiteExporter::export() gains an optional ?string $base parameter for specifying a base recipe to seed the export.

Technical details

The new route drupal_cms_helper.site_export (marked # @api in routing) maps to ExportController::exportArchive() (marked @internal). The controller reads the site_template_base state key, which the installer's SiteTemplateForm stores when a user chooses the drupal_cms_site_template_base recipe during setup. That path is forwarded to SiteExporter::export() as the new optional ?string $base parameter.

SiteExporter::export() was extended with a $base argument (defaulting to NULL). When a base path is supplied and is a valid directory, the new private copyBaseRecipe() method mirrors the base recipe into the destination (excluding its content/ directory) and renames *.example files by stripping the .example suffix, so scaffold templates become active. The logic that previously lived inline in SiteExportCommand was consolidated into copyBaseRecipe(). The .htaccess deletion from the exported config/ directory was also moved into SiteExporter::export() itself, making all callers benefit from it automatically.

An initial implementation added the export as a button on the core config export form via hook_form_config_export_form_alter, but was quickly reverted after UX review. The final design uses an invisible standalone route that site templates can surface where appropriate, and drops the earlier custom PharData class-exists access check in favor of the standard permission requirement alone.

Upgrade

Site template authors who call SiteExporter::export() directly can now pass a base recipe path as the second argument:

// Before (still works, $base defaults to NULL)
$siteExporter->export($destination);

// After: optionally seed the export from a base recipe
$siteExporter->export($destination, '/path/to/base-recipe');

To expose the export route in a custom site template, add a menu link entity pointing to drupal_cms_helper.site_export. No code changes are required to use the route; a user with administer site configuration can already access /admin/config/development/site-export directly.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 23, 2026
  • First commit: February 23, 2026
  • Last commit: March 9, 2026 (13 days and 8 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • thejimbirch (Kanopi Studios)
  • penyaskito (Acquia)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)

Collaboration

The change took two distinct rounds to land. The first round merged quickly with a single approving review from thejimbirch, but phenaproxima reverted it hours later after pameeela flagged that the config management UI was a confusing home for the export feature. In the comment announcing the revert, phenaproxima immediately proposed the alternative: an invisible route that individual site templates could choose to surface with their own menu links. penyaskito suggested splitting the functionality into a separate site_template_builder module, but phenaproxima preferred keeping it inside drupal_cms_helper alongside existing dev tooling. pameeela approved the revised route-based approach, and the implementation was re-landed in that form.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3574664: The `site:export` command should be able to export on top of another recipe
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Closed (fixed)
  • Changes: 5 files, +127/−12 · php +127/−12
  • Diff: 8670479
  • Discussion: #3574664 · 1 contributor · 13 comments
  • Followers: 1

Summary

The drush site:export command in Drupal CMS gains a --base option that lets site template authors seed an export from an existing recipe directory (such as drupal_cms_site_template_base). The base recipe's files are copied to the export destination first, preserving scaffolding like CI configuration, license files, and README documents, then the site's live configuration is exported on top. Three export bugs are also fixed: the command now refuses to overwrite an existing destination directory, the system.site front page path is converted from its internal system path to its URL alias in the exported config, and child menu link content entities now correctly record their parent menu link as a dependency in the exported content.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

In SiteExportCommand, the new --base option triggers a two-step copy before the export. First, Symfony\Component\Finder\Finder selects all files from the base recipe path with ignoreVCS(TRUE), ignoreDotFiles(FALSE) (so hidden files like .gitlab-ci.yml and .tugboat are included), and notPath('content') (so base recipe content is excluded). Symfony\Component\Filesystem\Filesystem::mirror() writes those files to the destination. Second, a follow-up Finder pass locates any file whose name matches *.example or .*.example and renames each by stripping the eight-character .example suffix, activating dormant placeholder files such as .gitattributes.example. The command also checks for an existing destination directory upfront and returns Command::FAILURE immediately if it exists.

GenericConfigurationListener is changed from final readonly to final (mutable) so that SiteExporter can toggle a public $convertFrontPagePathToAlias flag at runtime. When the flag is TRUE, the listener's __invoke() method calls AliasManagerInterface::getAliasByPath() on the system.site page.front value before writing it to config storage, replacing the internal node path with its URL alias. AliasManagerInterface is injected via the updated constructor. This shim is marked for removal when core issue #1503146 is released.

DefaultContentSubscriber becomes final readonly and gains an EntityRepositoryInterface constructor dependency. In preExport(), when the entity being exported is a MenuLinkContentInterface and its parent ID starts with menu_link_content:, the subscriber extracts the UUID, loads the parent entity via EntityRepositoryInterface::loadEntityByUuid(), and calls $event->metadata->addDependency() to register it. This shim is marked for removal when core issue #3562072 is released.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 19, 2026
  • First commit: February 19, 2026
  • Last commit: February 27, 2026 (7 days and 4 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • andyg5000 (Dripyard)
  • vishalkhode (Acquia)

Collaboration

The issue body credits Andy from Dripyard for reporting the export bugs affecting site template creators. phenaproxima authored and self-merged the fix, noting in the thread that strong test coverage and the need to keep site template work unblocked gave him confidence to merge without external review.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3575789: If there's only one site template in the code base, choose it automatically
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 5 files, +82/−83 · php +82/−83
  • Diff: 8f0de40
  • Discussion: #3575789 · 1 contributor · 6 comments
  • Followers: 2

Summary

The Drupal CMS installer now automatically selects a site template if only one is available in the codebase. Previously, the installer always displayed a selection form even when only a single template was present. This streamlines the installation process by skipping the unnecessary selection step. This change prepares for a marketplace workflow where users could download a pre-configured project with a single site template already chosen, then proceed directly through installation without redundant selection prompts.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The drupal_cms_installer_choose_template() task was converted from a simple form task to a custom function. This function scans for site templates using RecipeHandler::scan('Site') and stores them in install state. If exactly one recipe is found, it temporarily sets $install_state['interactive'] to FALSE before calling install_get_form(), which causes the form to submit programmatically without user interaction.

The RecipeHandler::scan() method was refactored from returning a raw Finder object to yielding Recipe objects directly, with optional filtering by recipe type. This moves error handling and recipe validation into the service layer. The method now catches RecipeFileException and displays errors, simplifying callers.

The SiteTemplateForm receives pre-scanned recipes from install state rather than scanning itself. The administrator role recipe is now enqueued earlier in the task function rather than on form submit. The default selection is set to the first recipe in the array, which is always drupal_cms_starter due to custom sorting logic.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 25, 2026
  • Committed: February 26, 2026 (1 day later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

phenaproxima identified the need to support a streamlined marketplace download workflow and implemented the automatic selection behavior within a single day. The change maintains backward compatibility with Drupal CMS's default multi-template setup while enabling future single-template distributions.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3573304: Create a 'Blank' template option for users who want to start from scratch
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +8/−6 · php +5/−5 · yml +3/−1
  • Diff: 85790f5
  • Discussion: #3573304 · 4 contributors · 24 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Drupal CMS now offers a "Blank" template option during installation for users who want to start with a minimal foundation. This template provides Drupal CMS's baseline functionality without the Mercury design system or component library, giving developers complete freedom to build their own design approach. The blank template includes an automatically generated minimal theme with just header, content, and footer regions, plus a single blank Canvas page as the home page.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The blank template is implemented by making drupal_cms_site_template_base visible and usable during installation. Previously, this site template was hidden from the installer UI and only available to developers via command line.

The implementation leverages the site_template_helper Composer plugin to dynamically generate a minimal theme called "blank" when the template is installed. This theme is based on core's starterkit_theme but includes only three regions: header, content, and footer. The theme generation happens automatically at composer require time through plugin configuration in the site template's composer.json.

The drupal_cms_installer_install_tasks_alter() function was simplified by removing the SiteTemplateFormAlter class that previously hid drupal_cms_site_template_base from the installer. The template now appears alongside other options like Starter and the various prebuilt templates.

The installer UI received CSS improvements to accommodate the additional template option, switching from a fixed three-column grid to a responsive grid that adapts from two to four columns based on viewport width. The SiteExporter service was updated to remove development-only dependencies like site_template_helper when exporting site configurations.

The blank template includes a single empty Canvas page set as the front page to avoid a 404 on first visit. It also includes the utility page content type through drupal_cms_content_type_base, which provides the Canvas page functionality.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 13, 2026
  • First commit: February 16, 2026 (3 days later)
  • Last commit: February 18, 2026 (2 days and 4 commits later)

Key contributors

  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • lauriii (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

phenaproxima implemented the technical approach after pameeela identified the need and lauriii provided requirements feedback. phenaproxima resurrected and integrated the site_template_helper plugin to enable dynamic theme generation, solving the challenge of scaffolding a minimal theme without maintaining another static theme in the codebase.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3574009: Add a hidden Drush command to export all content
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 6 files, +138/−35 · php +138/−35
  • Diff: 8a6b09b
  • Discussion: #3574009 · 1 contributor · 6 comments
  • Followers: 2

Summary

Drupal CMS now includes a hidden Drush command that makes it easy for developers to export all content from a site to a directory. This is useful for creating development snapshots, migrating content between environments, or backing up site content in a portable format. The command is hidden because it's intended for developer use rather than routine site administration. The change also extracts the content loading logic into a reusable component that both the site export and content export commands can use.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

This change adds a new content:export:all Drush command in the drupal_cms_helper module. The command is marked as hidden (not shown in normal help listings) and exports all content entities to a specified directory using Drupal's default content export system.

The implementation extracts the content loading logic from SiteExporter::loadAllContent() into a new ContentLoader class that implements IteratorAggregate. The ContentLoader iterates through all entity types, filters out internal entities and path aliases, and yields all content entities. It excludes users 0 and 1 since those always exist with the same IDs.

The new ExportAllContentCommand class uses the Exporter service to export each entity with its dependencies to the target directory. The command accepts a single required argument for the destination directory path.

Both ContentLoader and ExportAllContentCommand are marked as @internal, but the command itself is marked as @api to signal that developers can rely on it. The change also adds @api annotations to existing commands (content:import, site:export) to clarify their stability guarantees within Drupal CMS.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: February 17, 2026
  • Committed: February 17, 2026

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

phenaproxima identified the use case and implemented the feature in a single day, including extracting the shared content loading logic into a reusable component and updating the existing site export functionality to use it.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3569529: Add an AGENTS.md file
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 5 files, +134/−0 · md +134
  • Diff: aecaffb
  • Discussion: #3569529 · 4 contributors · 12 comments
  • Followers: 13

Summary

Drupal CMS now includes an AGENTS.md file to help AI coding assistants work more effectively with the codebase. This file provides structured guidance on the project's technology stack, coding standards, development commands, and prohibited actions. The documentation helps AI tools avoid common mistakes and follow project conventions when assisting developers with Drupal CMS contributions.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The new AGENTS.md file documents Drupal CMS-specific conventions for AI coding assistants. It covers the monorepo structure with recipes in the recipes directory, the drupal_cms_helper module, and the project_template directory used for end-user installations.

The file provides command references for common development tasks using DDEV and Drush, including recipe application, PHPUnit testing, site installation, and content export with drush content:export. It specifies coding standards like using strict typing, the ?type nullable syntax, #[\Override] attributes, and marking new classes as final with private members.

Several README files in protected directories (cpanel_template, patches, scripts, splitter) now explicitly warn agents not to modify those areas. The prohibited actions section lists destructive operations like running ddev tag or composer update outside the project root, and prevents agents from adding patches or pinning dependencies in Composer files.

The documentation emphasizes defensive programming in drupal_cms_helper, requiring checks like \Drupal::moduleHandler()->moduleExists() before using optional module functionality. It also instructs agents to mark new non-test classes as internal with specific warning text.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 25, 2026
  • Committed: February 4, 2026 (9 days and 2 commits later)

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • nod_ (Très Bien Tech)
  • marcus_johansson (FreelyGive)
  • thejimbirch (Kanopi Studios)
  • rajab natshah (Vardot)

Collaboration

This issue demonstrates the Drupal CMS team's pragmatic approach to AI tooling. The issue author acknowledged ambivalence about AI while recognizing its practical utility and the need to accommodate developers using these tools. Multiple contributors provided feedback to improve structure and readability, with discussion about whether to manage the file through a separate package like the scaffold system. The team decided keeping AGENTS.md in the repository made more sense for documenting internal workings.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3557383: Ship local video media type with Drupal CMS for better compatibility with Canvas
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal task
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3557383 · 3 contributors · 15 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

The drupal_cms_remote_video recipe now also ships the local video media type, so Canvas components that reference a video schema can use the media library picker instead of falling back to a less reliable file picker. The recipe description has been updated to reflect that it now covers both locally and remotely hosted video. Additionally, whitespace-to-underscore filename sanitization is enabled for uploaded files, and the built-in files view is disabled to avoid confusing site editors who may not understand the difference between "Files" and "Media".

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root problem was that Canvas resolves a video field (via json-schema-definitions://canvas.module/video) through the local video media type. When that media type was absent, Canvas degraded to a generic file picker that was unreliable. The fix adds core/recipes/local_video_media_type to the recipes list in drupal_cms_remote_video/recipe.yml, alongside the existing core/recipes/remote_video_media_type. The recipe also installs the file and views modules explicitly. A new core.entity_form_display.media.video.* config action mirrors the existing remote video form display: it hides uid, langcode, path, and created, and enables the name field. file.settings is updated via simpleConfigUpdate to set filename_sanitization.replace_whitespace: true in both drupal_cms_remote_video and drupal_cms_media (the latter backported to the 1.2.x branch of drupal_cms_image as a bug fix). views.view.files is disabled, and authenticated users receive the delete own files permission. The composer.json description was updated from "Configures display options for remote video" to "Configures display options for locally and remotely hosted video." A follow-up is planned to explore a Composer package alias so the recipe can also be referenced as drupal/drupal_cms_local_video, since the current package name (drupal_cms_remote_video) no longer reflects its full scope.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 12, 2025
  • Committed: November 19, 2025 (6 days and 3 commits later)

Key contributors

  • mherchel
  • pameeela (Technocrat)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)

Collaboration

pameeela noted early in the thread that adding the local video media type had always been planned as optional support, but she saw no major reason to keep it optional and suggested simply depending on the core recipe. phenaproxima agreed, opened MR !685, and then flagged the awkward naming of drupal_cms_remote_video after the fact. pameeela acknowledged the naming issue but approved the change rather than holding it up, reasoning it was not worth blocking the fix.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3529159: Bring the installer's language selector back
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 6 files, +49/−1 · js +25 · php +8/−1 · twig +7 · css +6 · yml +3
  • Diff: 810d2fe
  • Discussion: #3529159 · 3 contributors · 13 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Drupal CMS 2.x now shows a language selector dropdown in the installer. When a language is chosen, the installer reloads with the selected langcode in the URL. The selector is hidden while batch installation tasks are running. Note that downloading .po translation files at install time is not yet implemented and is deferred to a future change in Recipe Installer Kit; this change covers the UI groundwork only.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New languages template variable (keyed by langcode, each with name and direction) available in install-page.html.twig of drupal_cms_installer_theme

Technical details

The work is entirely within the drupal_cms_installer_theme sub-theme. The drupal_cms_installer_theme_preprocess_install_page() function in drupal_cms_installer_theme.theme was added to populate a $variables['languages'] array. It iterates LanguageManager::getStandardLanguageList() and, after the follow-up commits, passes each language's native name ($language[1]) and text direction ($language[2] falling back to LanguageInterface::DIRECTION_LTR) as an associative array. The $variables['languages'] variable is only populated when batch_get() returns empty, suppressing the dropdown during active install tasks.

The install-page.html.twig template conditionally renders a <select name="langcode"> element whose <option> tags carry the dir attribute for correct RTL rendering. A new Drupal.behaviors.languageSwitcher JavaScript behavior (in js/language-switcher.js) initialises the dropdown to the current langcode URL parameter (defaulting to en) and on change redirects to the same page with the updated parameter. CSS positions the selector absolutely at the top-right of the installer wrapper.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: June 9, 2025
  • Committed: June 9, 2025

Key contributors

  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • anjali rathod (Axelerant)
  • jose reyero (Cambrico)

Collaboration

phenaproxima credited anjali rathod, who authored the original proof-of-concept MR at drupalcmsmultilingual that the implementation is based on, and jose reyero, whose credit was transferred from the earlier duplicate issue #3513368.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.
